did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper uses an ensemble pretrained deep learning model for the classification of melanoma skin cancer images. Specifically, it fine-tunes three Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs)—VGG16, NASNetMobile, and ResNet50V2—and combines their decisions using ensemble learning and hard voting techniques to discriminate between benign and malignant skin lesions.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work involving the design and evaluation of an ensemble pretrained deep learning model (combining VGG16, NASNetMobile, and ResNet50V2) for classifying melanoma skin cancer images. It includes specific methodology (fine-tuning, ensemble learning, hard voting), a defined dataset (Kaggle MSC), and reports new empirical results (91.5% accuracy, 90.86% test accuracy, etc.). Although published in a conference proceedings (ICCIAA), it contains original research results rather than being a narrative or systematic review.

title: Design an Ensemble Pretrained Deep Learning Model for Classification of Melanoma Skin Cancer Images
journal: 2025 1st International Conference on Computational Intelligence Approaches and Applications (ICCIAA)
brief_summary: This study addresses the classification of melanoma skin cancer images by proposing an ensemble pretrained deep learning model to discriminate between benign and malignant lesions. The methodology involves fine-tuning three Convolutional Neural Networks—VGG16, NASNetMobile, and ResNet50V2—and combining their predictions using ensemble learning and hard voting techniques. The model was evaluated on the publicly available Kaggle Melanoma Skin Cancer (MSC) dataset, which contains approximately 10,000 images partitioned into training, validation, and testing sets. The primary evaluation metrics included accuracy, sensitivity, and specificity, with statistical significance assessed via unpaired t-tests. The proposed ensemble model achieved a test accuracy of 90.86% and a specificity of 95.20%, outperforming the individual base classifiers. The results demonstrate the efficacy of ensemble learning in augmenting diagnostic performance for melanoma detection without requiring extensive preprocessing or lesion segmentation.

lesion_types_studied.details: The study classifies images into two categories: benign (5500 images) and malignant (5105 images) from the Kaggle Melanoma Skin Cancer (MSC) dataset. The primary focus is on discriminating between benign and malignant melanoma skin lesions.

all_task_types.target: Benign vs. Malignant skin cancer labels
all_task_types.details: The paper performs binary classification to discriminate between benign (5500 images) and malignant (5105 images) melanoma skin cancer images using an ensemble of pretrained CNNs (VGG16, NASNetMobile, ResNet50V2) with hard voting. The model outputs discrete diagnostic categories rather than continuous values.

datasets_used.name: Kaggle Melanoma Skin Cancer (MSC) Dataset
datasets_used.description: A publicly available benchmark dataset obtained from the Kaggle repository, comprising approximately 10,000 skin cancer images with a size of 256x256x3 pixels.

use.training: Used for training the fine-tuned VGG16, NASNetMobile, and ResNet50V2 models (80% of the dataset).
use.validation: Used for validation to determine the optimal number of epochs (20 epochs) and monitor overfitting (10% of the dataset).
use.testing: Used for final evaluation of the ensemble model's accuracy, sensitivity, and specificity (10% of the dataset).

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To assess statistical significance of the performance differences between the proposed ensemble model and the individual base classifiers (VGG16, NASNetMobile, ResNet50V2).
To quantify the uncertainty or variability in the difference of means between the ensemble model and individual models during the t-test analysis.
To determine the statistical significance of the comparison results between the ensemble model and individual models (reported as < 0.0001).

hypothesis_testing.null_hypothesis: There is no significant difference in specificity between the proposed ensemble model and the individual pretrained base classifiers (VGG16, NASNetMobile, ResNet50V2).
hypothesis_testing.alternative_hypothesis: There is a significant difference in specificity between the proposed ensemble model and the individual pretrained base classifiers.
hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The unpaired t-test revealed a statistically significant difference (p-value < 0.001) in specificity between the proposed ensemble model and the individual pretrained models, indicating that the ensemble model's performance was significantly different (specifically higher, as noted in the results section) from the base classifiers.

Kaggle Melanoma Skin Cancer (MSC) Datase.notes: The ensemble model achieved a test specificity of 95.20%, surpassing all base classifiers by approximately 2.5%. The ensemble model's test accuracy (90.86%) was better than NASNetMobile by approximately 1%. The ensemble model's sensitivity (86.76%) was better than VGG16 but lower than the specificity values. Statistical significance was assessed via unpaired t-test with p-values < 0.0001 for all comparisons against individual models. ResNet50V2 reached the best validation accuracy of about 94% by the 14th epoch. VGG16 exhibited the lowest validation accuracy among the base models, achieving around 90% at epoch 18.

architecture_used.main_model: Ensemble of three pretrained Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs): VGG16, NASNetMobile, and ResNet50V2, combined via hard voting (majority voting).
architecture_used.pretraining: Pretrained on ImageNet (implied by 'pretrained models' and standard usage of these architectures), adapted via transfer learning for skin cancer classification.

architecture_details.modification_strategy: Selective unfreezing and fine-tuning. Global Average Pooling (GAP) layer added between convolutional layers. New dense layer with ReLU activation added. Dropout layer added to mitigate overfitting.

layer_freezing_details.ResNet50V2: First 180 layers frozen; final 10 layers adjusted.

architecture_details.ensemble_method: Hard voting (majority voting) to aggregate predictions from the three base classifiers.

training_details.loss_function: Not explicitly named, but Softmax activation with two output nodes implies categorical cross-entropy for binary classification.
training_details.activation_function: Softmax (final classification layer with 2 output nodes for benign vs. malignant)

layers: Final Dense layer with Softmax activation (2 nodes)

machine_learning_or_deep_learning_models: Proposed Ensemble Model (VGG16 + NASNetMobile + ResNet50V2 with hard voting)
preprocessing_techniques: No extensive preprocessing was applied; the study explicitly notes that the ensemble model's strength lies in its ability to achieve balanced performance without dependence on extensive preprocessing or lesion segmentation.
Input images were resized to 256x256x3 pixels.
conclusions: The proposed ensemble pretrained deep learning model, combining VGG16, NASNetMobile, and ResNet50V2 via hard voting, effectively discriminates between benign and malignant melanoma skin cancer images.
The ensemble model achieved a test accuracy of 90.86% and a specificity of 95.20%, outperforming the individual base classifiers (VGG16, NASNetMobile, and ResNet50V2).

The ensemble approach significantly augmented diagnostic performance, particularly in identifying negative classes (malignant lesions), with specificity surpassing all base classifiers by approximately 2.5%.
The model demonstrated robustness and resilience by aggregating outputs from multiple classifiers to reduce the impact of individual base model misclassifications.
The study highlights the efficacy of transfer learning and ensemble learning in skin cancer diagnosis, noting that the method does not require extensive preprocessing or lesion segmentation.
The lower sensitivity (86.76%) compared to specificity is attributed to the low inter-class distance and high intra-class variability inherent in medical image classification.
Statistical analysis using unpaired t-tests confirmed that the performance differences between the ensemble model and individual base classifiers were statistically significant (p < 0.0001).
important_remarks: The study relies exclusively on the Kaggle Melanoma Skin Cancer (MSC) dataset, which limits external validation and generalizability to other clinical populations or imaging modalities.
The dataset contains approximately 10,605 images, which is considered a small sample size for deep learning, potentially leading to overfitting and poor generalization to unseen cases.
The paper acknowledges class imbalance issues inherent in skin cancer datasets, which can negatively impact the classification of underrepresented classes.
The ensemble model does not utilize lesion segmentation or extensive preprocessing, which may introduce noise from background skin or non-lesion areas into the classification process.

The primary limitation cited is the difficulty in determining optimal hyperparameters (e.g., batch size, learning rate) for fine-tuning pretrained models, suggesting a lack of rigorous hyperparameter optimization.
The model was only trained on three specific pretrained architectures (VGG16, NASNetMobile, ResNet50V2), limiting the exploration of other potential state-of-the-art models.
VGG16 exhibited significant overfitting, with a large discrepancy between training accuracy (90.24%) and testing accuracy (79.92%), indicating instability in individual base models.
The reported test accuracy varies slightly between the abstract (91.5%) and the results section (90.86%), requiring clarification on the exact evaluation protocol.
The study lacks comparison with human dermatologists or other baseline clinical metrics, limiting the assessment of clinical relevance and utility.

No data leakage prevention strategies (e.g., patient-level splitting) are explicitly detailed, raising concerns about potential data leakage if images from the same patient appear in both training and testing sets.
